title: taskwrite.py: non-atomic overwrite of open-tasks.md risks board corruption on crash
evidence: taskwrite.py lines 105-106: `with open(board_path, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="\n") as f: f.write(new)`. The board is read into memory, transformed purely in memory by _taskboardlib functions, then written back with a plain truncate-and-write open. There is no write-to-temp-file-then-rename pattern. A process kill, power failure, or out-of-disk-space condition between the open (which truncates the file) and the completion of f.write() leaves open-tasks.md empty or partially written. The prior content is already gone at that point (the `"w"` mode truncates on open), so recovery requires restoring from git history.
impact: open-tasks.md is the task board read at every SessionStart and by statusline.py. A corrupt/empty board silently shows 0 in-flight tasks and loses all queued/in-progress task records until a human notices and restores from git. No automatic recovery path exists.
recommendation: Use an atomic write pattern: write to a sibling temp file (e.g. `open-tasks.md.tmp`), then `os.replace(tmp, board_path)`. `os.replace()` is atomic on POSIX and near-atomic (same-volume rename) on Windows, so a crash never leaves the board truncated. The same pattern should be applied to any state file written by a similar read-modify-write.
acceptance_criteria: taskwrite.py writes to a temp file and uses os.replace() to commit
A simulated crash (KeyboardInterrupt injected after open()) leaves the original file intact
init-codearbiter.py and other state writers use the same atomic pattern or are noted as crash-safe because they only create files (never overwrite existing ones)

title: migration-pass.py and security-pass.py write markers non-atomically; partial write keeps backstop fail-closed but forces gate re-run
evidence: migration-pass.py lines 91-92: `with open(marker, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f: f.write("\n".join(digests) + ...)`. security-pass.py lines 84-85 follow the identical pattern for the security-gate-passed marker. Both use a plain truncate-and-write open with no temp-file-rename. The backstop (pre-bash.py) reads the marker with `set(f.read().split())` — a partial hex digest in the file produces an unrecognized token that does not match any committed digest, so the backstop remains fail-closed (blocks the commit). Impact is not data loss but a spurious block that requires re-running the gate review.
impact: A crash mid-write produces a partial marker. The next commit attempt is blocked (fail-closed is the correct behavior) and the developer must re-run the migration/security review gate to re-record a complete marker. No silent pass-through of an unreviewed migration/secret change. Low impact because correctness (fail-closed) is preserved; the cost is inconvenience.
recommendation: Apply the same os.replace() atomic-write pattern as migration-001's fix. Write digests to a sibling .tmp file, then os.replace() into place. This eliminates the spurious-block failure mode on crash.
acceptance_criteria: migration-pass.py and security-pass.py write markers via os.replace()
A simulated crash leaves no partial marker file; the backstop either finds a complete prior marker or no marker (both handled correctly)

title: pre-edit.py H-05 append-only guard bypassed when old_string is empty string
evidence: pre-edit.py lines 43-45: `old = ti.get("old_string", "") or ""` then `if not new.startswith(old):`. In Python, `any_string.startswith("")` is always True, so when old_string is the empty string (or absent from the payload), the guard unconditionally passes regardless of new_string's content. An Edit tool call with `old_string=""` targeting overrides.log, triage.log, or sprint-log.md would not be blocked by the hook. Whether the Edit tool itself accepts an empty old_string is a separate layer of defense, but the hook guard — the declared control for H-05 at the Edit boundary — has this gap.
impact: If the Edit tool accepts an empty old_string (behavior depends on Claude Code internals), an audit log could be rewritten rather than appended. If the Edit tool rejects empty old_string independently, this gap is mitigated by that tool-layer behavior. Confidence is 0.80 (not 1.0) because the Edit tool's own empty-old_string handling is not confirmed here. The bypass at the hook level is observed (line 45 is the exact guard).
recommendation: Add an explicit guard before the startswith check: `if not old: block("H-05", "Edit with empty old_string on an audit log is prohibited — use '>>' to append.")`. An empty old_string can never represent a verifiable pure append; blocking it outright is the correct safe default.
acceptance_criteria: pre-edit.py blocks an Edit with old_string='' targeting any audit log file
Existing legitimate appends (non-empty old_string, new_string starts with old) continue to pass

title: farm.ts validate() dereferences required task fields without null guard; crashes instead of validating gracefully
evidence: farm.ts validate() lines 1292-1297 access `t.test.path` (line 1292), `t.filesInScope` (line 1294), and `t.gate.commands` (line 1297) for every task. These are `required` in plan.schema.json but validate() does not guard against null/undefined before dereferencing. A hand-crafted or partially-written plan.json with `"test": null`, `"gate": null`, or `"filesInScope": null` causes `TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'path')` rather than a friendly error message. The error propagates to main's `.catch()` and exits 1, so no task runs — the fail-closed outcome is correct — but the error message is opaque and does not name which task or field is malformed. Importantly, there is no JSON Schema validation at runtime against plan.schema.json (line 1494: `JSON.parse(...) as Plan` is a TypeScript type assertion, not a runtime schema check).
impact: A malformed plan.json (null required field) produces a generic TypeError crash instead of a clear validation message naming the task and field. Developer experience cost; no silent mis-parse or partial execution — validate() throws before any worktree is created. Severity low because correctness (no execution) is preserved.
recommendation: Add null/undefined guards in validate() before dereferencing required fields: `if (!t.test || typeof t.test.path !== 'string') throw new Error('task ' + t.id + ': test.path is required')`. Optionally add a lightweight runtime schema check (e.g. using the existing plan.schema.json) or at minimum document that plan.json must be produced by the sanctioned writing-plans skill.
acceptance_criteria: validate() emits a named error (task id + field name) for each missing required field
A plan.json with test:null, gate:null, or filesInScope:null produces a readable error, not a TypeError stack trace
No behavioral change for well-formed plans
